topics.title: What is recursion and how does it work?
prerequisites: A function is a reusable block of code that can be called with arguments
A program must eventually stop — infinite loops crash programs
learning_objectives: Understand that a recursive function calls itself with a smaller version of the problem
Identify base cases (when to stop) and recursive cases (how to reduce the problem)
Recognize problems where recursion is a natural fit (trees, factorials, fractals)
common_misconceptions: Recursion is just a fancy loop
Recursion is always slower or less efficient than iteration
A recursive function can call itself without limit (forgetting the base case)
socratic_path: If I asked you to compute 5 factorial (5!), how would you do it step by step?
Notice that 5! = 5 × 4!. And 4! = 4 × 3!. Do you see a pattern?
If a function computing n! can call a function computing (n-1)!, when does that chain of calls have to stop?
What happens if you forget the stopping condition and the function keeps calling itself forever?

topics.title: What is Big O notation and why does it matter?
prerequisites: Programs can be fast or slow depending on how they are written
As input grows larger, the time a program takes may grow as well
Mathematical functions like n, n², and log(n) grow at different rates
learning_objectives: Understand Big O as a measure of how runtime scales with input size
Distinguish O(1), O(log n), O(n), O(n log n), O(n²) and recognize examples of each
Explain why constant factors are dropped and why Big O focuses on worst case
common_misconceptions: O(n²) is always slower than O(n) for any input size
Big O measures actual running time in seconds
The fastest algorithm always has the smallest Big O class
socratic_path: If you have a list of 1000 names and want to find one, how many names might you have to check?
If the list doubles to 2000, how many checks do you need now? What if it doubles again?
What if the list were sorted — could you use a smarter strategy than checking every name?
How would you describe an algorithm where doubling the input only adds one more step?

topics.title: What is a pointer and why does it matter?
prerequisites: Computer memory can be thought of as a very long list of numbered locations (addresses)
Different data types (int, float, char) take different amounts of memory
learning_objectives: Understand that a pointer is a variable that stores a memory address rather than a value
Explain how dereferencing a pointer accesses the value at that address
Recognize why pointers enable dynamic memory allocation and data structures like linked lists
common_misconceptions: A pointer is the same as the variable it points to
Pointers are only needed in old languages like C — modern languages do not use them
Copying a pointer copies the data it points to
socratic_path: If every house on a street has an address, what is the difference between knowing an address and knowing what is inside the house?
In memory, if a variable stores the value 42, what might a pointer to that variable store?
What happens if you follow a pointer to read the value it points to? What if the pointer is invalid?
Why would you want to pass an address to a function instead of a copy of the actual data?

topics.title: What is asynchronous programming and when do you need it?
prerequisites: Programs execute statements one at a time (sequentially) by default
Some operations take a long time to complete: network requests, file reads, database queries
A CPU can execute millions of instructions while waiting for a network response
learning_objectives: Understand that async programming lets a program do other work while waiting for slow operations
Distinguish between synchronous blocking, async callbacks, promises/futures, and async/await
Recognize the event loop as the mechanism behind non-blocking I/O
common_misconceptions: Async programming is the same as multi-threading
Async code always runs faster than sync code
Async/await eliminates the need to think about concurrency
socratic_path: If you order food at a restaurant, do you stand frozen at the counter until it is ready, or do you sit down and do other things?
In a program, if you request data from a server and it takes 2 seconds, what is the CPU doing during those 2 seconds in a blocking program?
How could you design a program that does useful work while waiting, rather than just waiting?
What problems might arise if many tasks are all running at once and they try to share the same data?

topics.title: What is object-oriented programming and why was it invented?
prerequisites: Programs are made of data (variables) and behavior (functions)
Large programs become hard to manage when data and functions are scattered everywhere
Modeling real-world concepts in code makes programs easier to reason about
learning_objectives: Understand classes as blueprints and objects as instances
Explain encapsulation (data + behavior together), inheritance (reuse), and polymorphism (same interface, different behavior)
Recognize why OOP was developed to manage complexity in large codebases
common_misconceptions: OOP is the only correct way to write programs
Inheritance is always the right way to reuse code
Encapsulation just means making variables private
socratic_path: If you were modeling a library in code, what real-world things would you need to represent?
A Book has a title, author, and ISBN. What actions (behaviors) can you perform on a Book?
If both Book and Magazine share some properties (title, publisher), how might you avoid writing the same code twice?
If you have a list of Publications and want to print each one's details, do you need to know if each is a Book or Magazine?
